title: Hoof trimming a sheep
subtitle: Catching, setting up, and trimming overgrown hoof horn on a small flock, before foot rot sets in.
excerpt: Sheep hooves grow continuously and need trimming every six to eight weeks in wet lowland conditions, less often on dry upland ground. Overgrown horn folds under the foot and traps mud and bacteria, the starting condition for foot rot. A routine trim takes five to ten minutes per sheep once the animal is caught and sitting.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Better Returns Programme: Lameness in Sheep (2011). https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk

content.text: Regular hoof trimming is the first line of defence against
that folds under the sole creates the anaerobic, muddy pocket where the bacteria that cause foot rot thrive. Trim before you see a problem, not after lameness appears.

Work the flock into a corner or a handling pen so the animals cannot scatter. A shepherd's crook laid across a sheep's neck leg slows it enough to grip with both hands. Grip just above the hocks (rear legs) or around the neck and shoulder (front approach). Do not grip by the wool alone: wool tears and the sheep escapes.
Setting the sheep on its rump
To put a sheep into the

, stand beside the sheep facing the same direction. Place one hand under the chin and the other on the far hip. Tip the sheep back and to the side in one smooth movement, guiding its back down between your knees. The sheep's weight rests on its tail and back. Most will stop struggling within thirty seconds once they realise they cannot get up.

Work one foot at a time. Remove any packed mud from the cleft between the two digits. Trim the overgrown horn on the outer edges back to roughly level with the sole: the healthy sole should be firm, off-white, and slightly concave. If the horn is folded under the sole, trim it back to where firm horn meets soft. Do not cut into pink or bleeding tissue. The two digits on each foot should sit flat and parallel when the trim is done.

attrs.body: Foot rot produces a distinctive sweet-sour foul smell when the affected horn is trimmed back. The tissue beneath overgrown horn is grey or discoloured rather than white. A sheep with foot rot is severely lame, often grazing on its knees. The infection spreads to other sheep through pasture contact; separate the animal and treat promptly with a vet-prescribed antibiotic foot spray or a foot-bath course.
attrs.title: What foot rot looks like

content.text: Stand the sheep on dry ground for ten minutes before returning it to wet pasture if possible, to let the trimmed horn harden slightly. Record the date and any observations (lame feet, discolouration, smell) in the holding register. Most small flocks benefit from a six-weekly trim cycle on wet lowland ground and an eight-weekly cycle in drier conditions.
